coredata.dc:title: Ultra-stable dye-sensitized graphene quantum dot as a robust metal-free photocatalyst for hydrogen production
coredata.dc:creator: Dinda, Diptiman
Park, Hyunho
Park, Soo Young
coredata.dc:description: Covalently attaching a visible light absorbing dye to the graphene quantum dot (GQD) was proven as a viable method to prepare single component metal-free photocatalyst for solar hydrogen production. Despite promising aspects with earlier reported Rhodamine dye attached GQD system, however, quick deterioration of its HER activity within several hours seriously limits its practical application. We have challenged this stability issue by combining the N/S hetero atom doping in GQD structure and molecular structural elaboration of organic dye. By employing 2,3-diaminophenazine (Phz) attached NS-co-doped GQD (Phz-NS_GQD) as a single component photocatalyst in this work, we could demonstrate a dye-sensitized system which sustained over two days without any deterioration (complete linearity up to 50 h was measured) of its HER activity. Higher Apparent Quantum Yield (AQY) values of 49% and 47.7% at 400, 450 nm wavelength respectively were well supported to its improved catalytic efficiency for H2 production. It was shown that the N,S–co doping and the highly efficient photoinduced electron transfer to GQD core facilitated by phenazine dye are responsible for the improved HER efficiency as well as outstanding photostability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot (GQD)
Heteroatom doping
Dye-sensitization
Single component photocatalyst
Hydrogen evolution reaction (HER)
Stability of catalyst

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: N and S doped GQD systems
Origin of superior activity: Electrochemical and photoelectrochemical studies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of NS_GQD (a and b) at different magnification and inset shows particle size distribution; (c) STEM image and (d) HRTEM image with lattice fringes of graphene.
(a) Comparison of low resolution XPS spectra of different hetero atom doped GQD systems; high resolution deconvoluted XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N1s and (d) S2p peak of NS co-doped GQD system.

Comparison of (a) UV–vis absorption spectra, (b) Tauc plot for band gap determination, (c) linear sweep voltammetry curves for conduction band edge calculation and (d) band diagram of different hetero atom doped GQD systems.
(a) FTIR spectra of NS_GQD and Phz attached NS_GQD; and (b) high resolution deconvoluted N1s spectra of Phz-NS_GQD system.

(a) Comparison of photocatalytic H2 production of Phz attached hetero atom doped different GQD systems; (b) wavelength dependent Apparent Quantum Yield (AQY) measurement and (c) long term HER stability test with Phz-NS_GQD system under visible light irradiation using TEOA as sacrificial electron donor.

(a) Mott-Schottky plot for conductivity measurement, (b) Transient photocurrent response at zero bias voltage under simulated solar irradiation, (c) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements: Nyquist plots at different applied voltages in dark (D) and simulated solar irradiation (L) and (d) energy diagram for photocatalytic HER of Phz-NS_GQD system.

head.ce:title: Ultra-stable dye-sensitized graphene quantum dot as a robust metal-free photocatalyst for hydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Supramolecular Optoelectronic Materials, Department of Material Science and Engineering, Seoul National University, 08826 Seoul, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Supramolecular Optoelectronic Materials, Department of Material Science and Engineering, Seoul National University, 08826 Seoul, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Advanced Materials (RIAM), Seoul National University, 08826 Seoul, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Institute of Advanced Materials (RIAM), Seoul National University, 08826 Seoul, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: (Present Address) Nanoplasmonics Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: (Present Address) Nanoplasmonics Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Chung-Ang University, 84 Heukseok-ro, Dongjak-gu, Seoul 06974, South Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Laboratory of Supramolecular Optoelectronic Materials, Department of Material Science and Engineering, Seoul National University, 08826 Seoul, South Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Material Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 2,3-diamino phenazine (Phz) sensitized NS-co-doped GQD acts as a visible light active single component carbon based photocatalyst to produce H2 from water without any deterioration up to two days. NS-doping and carboxyl group passivation by covalent linkage invoke n-type semiconductivity in GQD to obtain a high AQY value of 49% for H2 production. π-conjugated planar rigid structure of phenazine helps to transfer photo-induced electrons quickly from dye antenna to conduction band of GQD in making the system highly stable during photocatalysis.

Covalently attaching a visible light absorbing dye to the graphene quantum dot (GQD) was proven as a viable method to prepare single component metal-free photocatalyst for solar hydrogen production. Despite promising aspects with earlier reported Rhodamine dye attached GQD system, however, quick deterioration of its HER activity within several hours seriously limits its practical application. We have challenged this stability issue by combining the N/S hetero atom doping in GQD structure and molecular structural elaboration of organic dye. By employing 2,3-diaminophenazine (Phz) attached NS-co-doped GQD (Phz-NS_GQD) as a single component photocatalyst in this work, we could demonstrate a dye-sensitized system which sustained over two days without any deterioration (complete linearity up to 50 h was measured) of its HER activity. Higher Apparent Quantum Yield (AQY) values of 49% and 47.7% at 400, 450 nm wavelength respectively were well supported to its improved catalytic efficiency for H2 production. It was shown that the N,S–co doping and the highly efficient photoinduced electron transfer to GQD core facilitated by phenazine dye are responsible for the improved HER efficiency as well as outstanding photostability.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is one of the most efficient and inexpensive pathway for the conversion of solar energy into a storable and transportable form regarding the urgency of making safe and clean renewable energy sources for modern civilization [1–3]. Although noble metals as well as transition metal oxides, sulfides and phosphides have been explored as the photocatalyst during last few decades [4–7], quite high cost and scarcity, low light absorption, rapid charge recombination, toxicity and instability of those catalysts make scientists interested in developing inexpensive, earth abundant and highly stable carbon based photocatalyst for the large-scale hydrogen production [8–10]. In this regard graphene made a smart entry after its groundbreaking discovery in 2004 [11–13]. In earlier period, graphene or reduced graphene oxide sheets have been utilized extensively as the electron transport layer as well as supporting matrix to build a novel hybrid photocatalyst system because of its remarkable room-temperature mobility of charge carriers (2 × 105 cm2 V−1 s−1) and superior conductivity (106 S cm−1) [14–17]. After 2010, however, scientists started to explore oxidized graphene sheets i.e. graphene oxide (GO) and later on few nanometer sized graphene i.e. graphene quantum dot (GQD) as the promising carbon based active photocatalyst due to their tunable band gap, chemical stability and versatile functionalization compared to the state-of-the-art inorganic catalysts [18–21]. With easier chemical reactions, band gap of GQD can be precisely tuned by cutting micrometer sized graphene sheets to few nanometer GQD to control its catalytic activity [22,23]. Besides, GQD has remarkable up-conversion ability to convert low-energy photons into high-energy photons and exceptionally long lifetime of hot carriers [24,25]. These unique properties of GQD are believed to help photoinduced charges to separate and also improve photodegradation activity, making it a superior candidate as electron acceptor or photo absorber for solar water splitting [26,27].

Despite such a huge and important potential, GQD has rarely been explored for solar hydrogen production as it shows very weak visible light absorption. To address this issue, we have previously demonstrated a covalent dye-sensitization technique with Rhodamine dye attached GQD (Rho-GQD) for the successful visible light HER [28]. Rho-GQD showed quite noticeable HER performance, whilst bare GQD showed no HER activity at all under visible light irradiation. To realize the practical application, we have analyzed the remaining issues to be solved as follows. First, we have to further enhance HER performance of the photocatalyst. Second, the stability of the catalyst during long-term photocatalysis experiment should be secured. The latter is one of the major issues for dye-sensitization systems as the organic dye starts to degrade with time under light irradiation. Indeed, HER performance decreased to 23% of its initial HER activity in case of our previously reported Rho-GQD material after only 12 h of visible light irradiation. As a strategy for the first issue, we must increase visible light absorption as well as conductivity in GQD by some structural modification to enhance its HER performance. Recently, electron rich hetero atom doping in graphene or GQD became a very popular strategy to tune their band gap and conductivity for improved catalytic activity [29,30]. Following this strategy, we have doped nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) as well as both in GQD structure to tune its band gap. For the second issue of photostability, we came to consider that the photostability of organic dye may be directly related to the photo-induced electron transfer rate to the GQD, since the faster electron transfer means the immediate depopulation of the harmful excited state which otherwise should result in the dye degradation. According to this hypothesis, we decided to employ phenazine dye which have been reported to be an efficient photon antenna due to their π-conjugated planar rigid structure with electron rich substituents making it efficient sensitizer with rich redox chemistry and quick electron transfer property [31,32]. Particularly, we have selected amine based phenazine dye i.e. 2,3-diaminophenazine (Phz) after checking its energy level match with our GQD/doped-GQD systems. We have attached this phenazine dye covalently to the GQD/doped GQD systems to use them as metal-free single component catalysts for photocatalytic HER under visible light irradiation. By employing 2,3-diaminophenazine (Phz) attached NS-co-doped GQD (Phz-NS_GQD) as a single component photocatalyst in this work, we could demonstrate a very high HER activity of 1.44 µmol h−1 which sustained over two days without any deterioration (complete linearity up to 50 h was measured). It is to be noted that the HER activity and photostability of Phz-NS_GQD indeed make a new state-of-the-art among various carbon based metal-free photocatalysts.

Graphite powder and 2,3-diaminophenazine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 wt%), phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85 wt%), hydrogen chloride (HCl, 35%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), sodium nitrate (NaNO3) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Merck Chemicals. N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and 2-morpholin-4-ylethanesulfonic acid (MES) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All the chemicals were of analytical-grade and used without any further purification.
In the first step, we have synthesized GO from graphite powder with some modifications of the well-known Hummers method following our previously reported method [33].

In the second step, we have synthesized GQD as well as doped/co-doped GQD systems. For the preparation of GQD, we have taken 15 mL (3 mg/mL) of GO solution in a beaker and diluted it to 35 mL by adding distilled water. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 1 h for homogeneous dispersion. Then, the pH of mixture was adjusted to 8 by adding dropwise 2 N NaOH solution followed by further sonication for 1 h. Finally, this black solution was transferred into a Teflon lined autoclave and was treated at 200 °C for 20 h. After cooling to room temperature, the filtrate solution was collected using 0.2 µm Millipore membrane filter and was dialyzed by 1 KDa dialysis kit.

For the preparation of doped/co-doped GQD system, we have used urea, Na2S and both as the precursor for N, S and N, S co-doping, respectively. We have taken same amount of exfoliated GO dispersion and added 200 mg of urea or 300 mg of Na2S·9H2O or both to it followed by sonication for 30 min for the doping of N, S, and both, respectively. Then the same procedure as GQD was applied to make doped GQD systems.

In the final step, we have covalently attached 2,3-diaminophenazine (Phz) covalently to the GQD as well as doped GQD systems via amide bond formation reaction to make 2,3-diaminophenazine attached GQD (Phz-GQD), 2,3-diaminophenazine attached N-doped GQD (Phz-N_GQD), 2,3-diaminophenazine attached S-doped GQD (Phz-S_GQD) and 2,3-diaminophenazine attached N, S co-doped GQD (Phz-NS_GQD) systems. For this covalent bonding of phenazine dye, 10 mL (0.5 mg/mL) of as synthesized GQD/doped-GQD solution was diluted to 40 mL with distilled water. Then, 65 mg of MES buffer was added into it and followed by stirring for 30 min to maintain pH 6 during reaction. Then, 60 mg of EDC and 50 mg of NHS were sequentially added into it and the solution was stirred in dark. After 1 h, 30 mg of Phz was added into that solution and left for 24 h under dark for amide bond formation. After stopping the reaction, we have filtered the resulting mixture with Millipore membrane and collected bright yellowish colored solid residue after several washing with ethanol and water. Finally, we dried the residue in vacuum oven and kept it in dark. To investigate the effect of GQD co-doping in the case of already reported Rhodamine attached GQD system, we have prepared samples for GQD and NS_GQD systems attached with Rhodamine 123 dye under the identical reaction condition. We have also calculated wt% of attached phenazine to GQD system by comparing UV–Vis spectrum of phenazine (Fig. S1) at 425 nm wavelength. The calculated wt% of phenazine in Phz-NS-GQD system is 71% which is similar to the ∼ 78% Rhodamine content in our previous reported Rho-GQD sample (maximum HER was observed for the 64% sample).

We have characterized our dye-sensitized GQD or hetero atom doped GQD systems by using different spectroscopic as well as microstructural analysis. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were made by Nicolet iS50 (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and AXIS-HSi (KRATOS) systems, respectively. JEOL-JEM-2100F high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) was used for morphological and elemental analysis. UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence measurements were carried out by using Shimadzu UV-1650 PC and Cary Varian spectrophotometer, respectively. Fluo Time 200 spectrometer (Pico Quant) equipped with a Pico Harp300 TCSPC board and a PMA182 photomultiplier was used for fluorescence lifetime measurements.

For photocatalytic HER measurements, we have taken a fixed amount of the Phz-GQD in 50 mL of Pyrex vial and added water to it. Then the mixture was sonicated for making homogeneous solution. Then, 10 vol% triethanolamine (TEOA) as the sacrificial reagent was added to it. Total volume was maintained to 13 mL after adjusting the pH of the solution to 7 with 2 N HCl and NaOH solutions. After gas purging for 15 min by Ar mixed with 1% CH4, the sample vial was irradiated with 300 W xenon lamp using 400 nm cut-off filters to eliminate the UV light. We have calculated the amount of evolved H2 gas by Agilent 7890A gas chromatography (equipped with thermal conductivity detector and 5 Å molecular sieve column) during the progress of the photocatalytic measurements.

All the electrochemical and photoelectrochemical measurements were carried out by Vertex.One (Ivium technology) electrochemical analyzer using standard three electrode system with 0.1 M KPF6 aqueous solution as the supporting electrolyte. Here, we have used Phz-GQD or Phz-N/S/NS_GQD materials as the working electrode by drop casting them over ITO coated polyethylene terephthalate (PET) plates. Pt wire and Ag/AgCl were used as counter and reference electrode, respectively. Oxidation potential of 2,3-diaminophenazine (Phz) was measured by cyclic voltammetry using 0.01 MAgNO3/Ag as a reference electrode, glassy carbon disc (diameter = 3 mm) as a working electrode and Pt wire as a counter electrode. The redox potential of reference electrode was calibrated using ferrocenium/ferrocene (Fc+/Fc) as an internal standard. Here, 0.1 M NBu4PF6 in anhydrous acetonitrile was used as the supporting electrolyte. Solar simulator model PEC L01 with 100 mW cm−2 irradiance was used as the light source for photoelectrochemical measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N and S doped GQD systems
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of our GQD and doped/co-doped GQD was measured by TEM analysis. TEM images in Fig. 1 (a and b) represent unimodal distribution of N, S co-doped GQDs (NS_GQD) with average size of 2–4 nm (inset). The bright spots in Scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) image in Fig. 1(c) clearly shows homogeneous distribution of GQDs formed by hydrothermal cutting of micrometer sized GO as shown in Fig. S2. The size of NS_GQDs is almost similar to the undoped and N/S doped GQDs as shown in Figs. S3–S5. From the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image in Fig. 1(d), we can clearly observe the (1120) in-plane lattice of graphene with a d-spacing of 0.24 nm.

Elemental composition of the GQDs was characterized by XPS analysis. From the low resolution XPS curve in Fig. 2 (a), we can observe the presence of typical C1s, O1s, N1s, S2s and S2p peaks at 284, 532, 400, 228 and 166 eV respectively in NS_GQD sample. On the other hand, only the (C, O), (C, O, N), and (C, O, S) elements are present in GQD, N-GQD and S_GQD samples, respectively. This clearly indicates that we have successfully doped/co-doped N and S in GQD structure via hydrothermal treatment. We have deconvoluted the high resolution C1s spectra to analyze the different functional groups present in the systems. From the deconvoluted C1s spectra of NS_GQD in Fig. 2(b), we could observe peaks for C-C, C-S & C-N, C-O, C = O and O = C-OH respectively. This is clearly different from the deconvoluted C1s spectra of GQD in Fig. S6, which consist only of C-C, C-O, C = O and O = C-OH peaks i.e. oxygen containing functional groups with higher intensity. The appearance of new C-N/C-S peaks in NS_GQD unambiguously confirms the doping of both N and S atoms in GQD structure [34]. We have further analyzed high resolution N and S peaks to examine the type of N and S doping in NS_GQD sample. Three distinct peaks of pyridinic, pyrrolic and quaternary N were identified from the deconvoluted N1s spectra of NS_GQD in Fig. 2(c). Here, strong intensity of pyrrolic peak indicates that the doped N atoms are mainly located in the π conjugated system and contribute to the π system with two p-electrons that eventually helps to improve charge transport in NS_GQD [35]. From the high resolution S2p peak, we can see the presence of C-S-C bond along with oxidized S peak in Fig. 2(d). From the deconvoluted peaks, the intensities of oxidized S-peaks have higher intensity compared to C-S-C peak indicating the presence of sulfur groups at the edges of graphene in its oxidized form. This kind of edge-sulfurized functional groups have higher degree of polarity that can serve as highly active sites for catalytic reactions [36].

We have also measured the Raman spectra of GQD and doped GQD systems and calculated ID/IG ratio to understand the order of defect states in them. From Fig. S7, we can see that all the systems show typical D and G band at 1350 and 1590 cm−1 of graphene structure. In case of GQD, the intensity of D band is lower compared to G band resulting in the ID/IG ratio of 0.92. After doping N/S in it, D band intensity is increased to the ID/IG ratio of 1.09 and 1.04 for N and S-doped GQD, respectively, and further to 1.13 for NS-co-doped GQD system. Therefore, it is most likely that the structural defect states are generated by hetero atom doping and particularly maximized for co-doped system.

Further, we have studied absorption and fluorescence spectra of all the GQD systems to analyze their band structure. Our synthesized GQD sample mainly shows absorption in the UV region as shown in Fig. 3 (a) due to their very small sizes. After doping N/S to it, the absorption peak shapes were changed with the lower energy tails extending into visible region through added conjugation of the electron rich N and S atoms with the π orbital of graphene, which reportedly improved their catalytic activity under solar light [37]. Then, we could calculate band gap from the Tauc plot of optical absorption as shown in Fig. 3(b). It was clearly shown that the band gap energy was gradually reduced from 3.35 eV of GQD to 3.25 (N_GQD), 3.18 (S_GQD) and finally 3.1 eV for NS_GQD suggesting the more effective electron conjugation with N, S to N, S co-doping. We have also checked fluorescence spectrum to analyze their band narrowing. The maximum fluorescence peak was gradually red shifted as well from GQD (429 nm) to 456 nm for NS_GQD supporting their bandgap narrowing as shown in Fig. S8. Then, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) experiments were carried out to measure the conduction band (CB) edge of all the GQD samples as shown in Fig. 3(c). Apparently, there is only small changes in the energy of CB edge indicating less effect of heteroatom doping over π* orbital of graphene compared to the π orbital. From these CB edge energies and the optical band gaps measured by Tauc plot, we have constructed band energy diagram for our GQD samples as shown in Fig. 3(d). It is noted that the valance band of the N, S, and NS_GQD is gradually upshifted owing to the increasing electron conjugation and decreasing ionization energies of heteroaromatic groups.

To compare the conductivity of GQD samples, we have measured change in capacitance (C) with applied potential by electrochemical impedance analyzer for un-doped/doped GQD systems and drawn Mott-Schottky (M−S) plot with 1/C2 vs. applied potential. For virgin GQD sample, it shows only p-type conductivity with Fermi level (EF) at about +2.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl that probably originates from the electron trapping effects of oxygen containing carboxylic and hydroxylic groups as shown in Fig. S9 [28,38]. On the other hand, additional n-type semiconductor behavior is observed after doping N/S atom as depicted in Figs. S10 and S11 with Fermi level (EF) at −0.89, −0.80 V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively. Similar trend of n-p type ambipolar conductivity with n-type EF at −0.75 V vs. Ag/AgCl, is shown for N and S co-doping case in Fig. S12. It is thus speculated that the electron trapping sites might be passivated by N, S doping or n-type domains are created in GQD structure after these hetero atoms doping due to formation of pyrrolic/pyridinic N-structure for N-doping, -C-S-C/-C-SOx-C- structures for S-doping and both for NS co-doping system. It should be noted that the HER absolutely demands effective separation of photogenerated e-h pair and transport of electron carrier (n-type semiconductivity) under visible light irradiation thus rationalizing the NS-co-doping of GQD [28,30,39,40].

We have covalently attached 2, 3-diaminophenazine (Phz) dye to the GQD systems via amide bond formation between the carboxylic acid groups of GQD and primary amine of the phenazine dye. To confirm the covalent attachment, we have analyzed FTIR and XPS spectra for the dye attached GQD samples. From the FTIR spectrum of NS_GQD shown in Fig. 4 (a), we can see the characteristic peaks of C = C stretching vibration peak along with C-N and C-S stretching vibration peaks at 1605, 1330 and 1125 cm−1 respectively. From the Phz-NS_GQD spectrum, on the other hand, we can see additional new peaks at 1632, 3150 and 3300 cm−1 related to O = C-NH-, C-H stretching of phenazine and N-H stretching vibrations, respectively. Particularly, a strong peak at 1632 cm−1 is attributed to the amide linkage after covalent attachment of phenazine dye to NS-GQD system [41]. Additionally, we have deconvoluted high resolution C1s and N1s spectra of Phz-NS_GQD system to confirm covalent attachment. The deconvoluted N1s spectra in Fig. 4(b) shows extra peak of amide bond (-HN-C = O) that arises because of covalent attachment of phenazine to GQD [42]. From Fig. S13, we further notice that the peak intensity of C-N bond is increased at the cost of O = C/O = C-OH peaks. These results clearly confirm covalent attachment of phenazine dye to the NS-co-doped GQD system. Similarly, the presence of amide linkage in the FTIR data from Fig. S14 also confirms covalent attachment of Rhodamine dye to GQD surface in case of Rho-NS_GQD system.

We have employed these phenazine attached GQD systems as the active single photocatalyst for HER under visible light irradiation with 300 W Xenon lamp using 400 nm cutoff filter. We have done all the photocatalytic measurements taking a fixed 100 mg/L dose of catalyst by making a homogeneous solution and used 10 vol% triethanolamine (TEOA) as a sacrificial electron donor without using any additional co-catalyst. From Fig. 5 (a), it is shown that all our phenazine attached GQDs show completely linear increase of H2 production over hours, which contrasts to the problematic HER rate decrease reported for the Rhodamine attached GQD (Rho-GQD). It is also seen that the HER rate of Phz-GQD is 0.36 µmol h−1 already outperforming Rho-GQD (0.12 µmol h−1). However, even larger increase in the rate was observed by the doping of GQD to show 0.91 µmol h−1 by N-doping and 1.09 µmol h−1 by S-doping, respectively. When we have used co-doped Phz-NS_GQD system, HER activity is amplified to 1.44 µmol h−1 while keeping the non-decaying rate behavior. To know the effect of dye sensitization independently, we have also measured HER performance under visible light irradiation with control GQD samples (GQD, N_GQD, S_GQD, NS_GQD) without phenazine attachment. We observed no noticeable H2 evolution for all these samples, undoubtedly due to the too small absorption coefficients of them in the visible region. Therefore, attaching phenazine as photon antenna, all the GQD based systems became active under visible light and showed completely linear HER activity without further addition of any noble metal co-catalyst.

As our doped GQD systems show n-p type ambipolar semiconducting behavior, they are likely to be more active in separating photo-generated e-h pair resulting in the superior HER activity compared to the un-doped GQD system [39,40]. Moreover, the electron conjugation of N2p/S3p orbitals with the graphene π network should provide an improved interaction of C-site with H+ making GQD more efficient in photocatalysis [43–45]. Besides, the possible presence of oxidized -C-SOx-C- peaks in S_GQD system is known to help photogenerated charge separation more effectively to enhance HER activity [46]. It was also reported that the simultaneous doping of S and N in the graphene network produces a synergistic effect that allows precise tuning of ΔGH* to achieve an outstanding HER activity, which is attributed to the asymmetric spin and charge density around the carbon sites [34,47]. To further confirm these effects of N/S doping in HER activity, we have also synthesized the Rhodamine-attached GQD and N, S-co-doped GQD samples using the same reaction conditions adopted for the phenazine attached ones. Consistent with the Phz-NS_GQD cases, significant enhancement in HER activity was observed by using the NS_GQD over GQD (see Fig. S15) reconfirming the general utility of N/S-doping in GQD structure. For these Rho-NS-GQD systems, however, the HER curve is yet saturating to show the significant degradation of HER activity over hours.

We have calculated wavelength dependent Apparent Quantum Yield (AQY) values with the most effective Phz-NS_GQD sample to get more insight into its HER activity. It shows AQY values of 49, 47.7 and 1.6% at 400, 450 and 500 nm light irradiation, respectively which are well agreement with its absorption spectrum as shown in Fig. 5(b). All the calculations for AQYs are given in the supporting information. Such a high AQY value for Phz-NS_GQD clearly confirms its superior HER activity that is generated by the covalent attachment of phenazine dye photon antenna followed by N and S atom doping in GQD.

Besides the photocatalytic activity, photostability is no less important issue for dye-sensitized systems as the dye starts to degrade with extended light exposure resulting in the deterioration of HER performance. One should carefully screen the appropriate dyes to make a stable photocatalytic system aiming at its commercial application of hydrogen production. In case of our previously reported Rho-GQD system, the HER rate decreased to 23% of its initial rate only after 12 h of photocatalytic experiment. We could address this issue by finding a stable phenazine based dye i.e. 2,3-diaminophenazine as the photon antenna with its relevant energy levels well aligned with those of GQDs according to the LSV/CV experiments (vide infra). As shown in Fig. 5(c), it is noteworthy that our Phz-NS_GQD as a single component photocatalyst shows non-decreasing HER activity even after 50 h of long photocatalytic experiment. Such enhanced stability of Phz-NS_GQD over Rho-NS_GQD is most likely attributed to the faster electron transfer from phenazine to NS_GQD to immediately drain out its excited state and also to the inherent stability of the dye itself. It was indeed reported that two pyridinic N atoms comprising the conjugated structure of phenazine dye facilitates photoexcited electron transfer [31,32].

To further check the photostability of the catalyst, we have measured XRD, XPS and Raman analysis of the sample after 50 h of HER experiment. There is no such noticeable change in the XRD spectra of Phz-NS_GQD after HER as shown in Fig. S16. In case of low resolution XPS spectra in Fig. S17(a), still we can observe the peaks of O, N and S atoms prominently after HER measurements although their intensities are slightly decreased. We have further deconvoluted N1s spectra to get more details of its stability. Fig. S17(b) clearly shows the presence of strong amide linkage in the sample even after 50 h of light irradiation. These observations confirm higher photostability of Phz-NS_GQD system under visible light exposure that makes the system superior in HER activity. We have also measured Raman spectrum of the sample before and after HER experiment. Both the spectrum shows higher D band intensity compared to G band owing to N and S doping in GQD structure. The ID/IG ratios are quite similar of 1.02 and 1.05 before and after HER experiment respectively as shown in Fig. S18. This observation stoutly confirms the rigidity of the GQD structure even after long light exposure during photocatalysis experiment that eventually helps to get such linear HER activity for Phz-NS_GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Origin of superior activity: Electrochemical and photoelectrochemical studies
ce:section.ce:para: Combined electrochemical and photoelectrochemical studies were made to understand the origin of superior photocatalytic HER activity of Phz-NS_GQD. First, we have carried out the Mott-Schottky analysis of NS_GQD as mentioned earlier. It is seen that the virgin GQD showed only p-type semiconductor behavior with Fermi level (EF) at about +2.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl not to give any HER efficiency consistent with our earlier publication [28]. On the other hand, the N-, S-, and NS- doped GQD additionally showed n-type semiconducting behavior with associated n-type EF at −0.89, −0.80, and −0.75 V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively. Therefore, these N/S-doped GQDs are potentially good photocatalyst for HER but with inherent deficiency of visible light absorption as demonstrated by their negligible HER efficiency (vide supra). Then, we analyzed Mott-Schottky (M−S) plot of Phz-NS_GQD system as shown in Fig. 6 (a). It also shows typical ‘n’ type semiconductor behaviors like NS_GQD system along with more lowering (positive potential shifting) of the EF level to −0.68 V vs Ag/AgCl that leads to better electron transfer, which eventually helps to improve charge separation during photocatalytic HER [48,49]. Previously, we have shown that the carboxylic groups in GQDs are electron trap sites which can effectively be passivated by covalent attachment of organic dye via amide bond formation to restore n-type conductivity in it [28]. Using phenazine dye as covalently-bonded photon antenna, we have successfully activated N/S-doped GQD systems for photocatalytic HER under visible light by combining both the N/S-doping and dye passivation effect for synergetic increase of n-type semiconductivity. To further confirm this, we have also studied M−S plot of Rhodamine attached NS_GQD sample as shown in Fig. S19. Similarly, further lowering of EF to −0.65 V Ag/AgCl is also observed in Rho-NS_GQD in contrast to the EF of −0.85 V Ag/AgCl observed in Rho-GQD [28], rationalizing the higher HER performance of the former over the latter (vide supra).

Transient photocurrent measurement was done using Phz-NS_GQD as active electrode under simulated solar light illumination with UV cut-off filter along with Pt as counter and Ag/AgCl as reference electrode in 0.1 M KPF6 electrolyte solution. A prominent and reproducible photocurrent response was observed at zero bias voltage when we turn on the light as shown in Fig. 6(b). It shows quite the same photocurrent response even after 30 repetitive cycles confirming its high stability during photocatalysis as reflected in HER measurements. Here, negative photocurrent response of Phz-NS_GQD system also indicates its photocathodic behavior for possible utilization in photoelectrochemical H2 production under visible light irradiation. This kind of observation clearly implies decent charge separation and better electron conductivity in Phz-NS_GQD making it a superior photocatalyst under visible light.

To gain more insight into photoelectrochemical properties, we have also checked the change in charge transfer resistance in the absence and presence of light by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis for Phz-NS_GQD system. From the Nyquist plot in Fig. 6(c), we see the narrowing of semi-circle going from 0 V to −0.6 V indicating decrease in charge transfer resistance. Decrease in charge transfer resistance is associated with better charge separation by external bias voltage making it more conducting. Phz-NS_GQD shows comparatively lower charge transfer resistance compared to dark at open circuit voltage and gradually decreased with higher applied voltage. Here, visible light irradiation makes this change more pronounced to develop a stable photocatalyst. Thus, doping hetero atom followed by dye sensitization with planar phenazine structure helps to separate charges efficiently resulting in such decreased charge transfer resistance in visible light making Phz-NS_GQD an efficient and stable photocatalyst for solar hydrogen production [50].

To understand the mechanism of such superior catalytic activity of this Phz-NS_GQD system, we have separately measured energy levels of phenazine molecule from its UV–Vis and cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement as shown in Figs. S20, S21(a, b) and S22, respectively. This was done because the phenazine dye and NS-GQD are electronically isolated whilst being tethered covalently via non-conjugated amide bond. We have calculated the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) positions of phenazine as −5.7 and −3.1 eV (vs. vacuum) respectively. Then, we could construct energy band diagram of Phz-NS_GQD system to analyze H2 production as depicted in Fig. 6(d). After visible light absorption by phenazine, the excited electrons should efficiently be transferred from the LUMO of phenazine to the CB of NS_GQD possibly by either the reductive quenching or oxidative quenching mechanism [51]. To know which mechanism is operating in our Phz-NS_GQD, we have measured the fluorescence lifetime data of Phz, Phz-NS_GQD and its change after addition of TEOA at 570 nm emission wavelengths. From Fig. S23 and Table S1, it is clearly seen that the lifetime of phenazine does not change even after covalent attachment to the NS_GQD, which suggests the oxidative electron transfer does not happen in this system. On the other hand, the fluorescence lifetime is gradually decreased, and the fluorescence intensity is significantly quenched in proportion to the amount of TEOA to Phz-NS_GQD as shown in Fig. S24 and Fig. S25, respectively (see also Table S1). Therefore, we can conclude that the reductive quenching mechanism is working in our Phz-NS_GQD system. Briefly, phenazine dye is first excited by visible light and TEOA molecules donate electrons to the excited phenazine molecule making it to radical anion (Phz*‾) followed by fast electron transfer to the active catalyst NS_GQD.

We have successfully synthesized and characterized nano-sized (2–4 nm) and N/S-doped GQDs with covalently bonded phenazine dyes as the single component photocatalyst for HER. Phz-NS_GQD showed non-decreasing H2 production rate over two days without using any co-catalyst under visible light irradiation. NS-doping of GQD and carboxyl group passivation by phenazine dye are considered to make synergetic increase of the n-type semiconductivity of GQD to endow such HER activity with a maximum AQY of 49% at 400 nm wavelength. Fast electron transfer from Phz to NS_GQD via reductive quenching mechanism was found to be responsible for such outstanding HER efficiency and photostability of Phz-NS_GQD.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the NRF funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (2017R1E1A1A01075372[RIAM0417-20210043]).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcat.2021.09.032.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
